title: What I Learned from Anthropic’s “Claude Code in Action” Course
author: Naman Verma
text:
What I Learned from Anthropic’s “Claude Code in Action” Course
I recently sat through the Claude Code in Action course by Anthropic, and I’ve gathered the heavy-hitting takeaways so you don’t have to.
Well, actually — you probably should still take it. It’s short, teaches you the fundamental “brain” of a coding assistant, and you get a certificate to show off on LinkedIn. But if you want the “too long; didn’t read” version of how this tool actually changes your workflow, here is the breakdown.
The Anatomy of an Assistant
We often treat AI like a magic text box, but Claude Code follows a very logical three-step loop:
- Gather Context: It looks at what you have.
- Make an Action Plan: It decides what needs to change.
- Take Action: It executes.
It does this through a set of tools — literally reading and writing to your files. While something like Cursor is an incredible IDE for your day-to-day, targeted coding, Claude Code feels built for the “big picture.” It’s exceptionally good at traversing a massive codebase to understand where everything lives.
Pro Tips for Getting Started
If you’re going to use it, don’t just start typing. You need to “prime the engine” first:
- The
/init
Command: Run this first. It scans your codebase and generates aclaude.md
file. This acts as Claude’s "map" of your project. - Custom Context: You can manually add features or quirks of your codebase to that
.md
file. For private notes, useclaude.local.md
. - Memory Management: Use the # symbol in conversation to pin specific points to Claude’s memory. If the chat gets too long, run
/compact
to summarize the history so Claude doesn't lose the thread.
The “Modes” of Problem Solving
One of the coolest things I learned was how to toggle Claude’s “brain.” These modes can be used separately or together to get exactly what you want:
- Planning Mode: Use this for breadth. If the task involves touching 15 different files and multiple features, this is your go-to.
- Think Mode: Use this for depth. If you have a logic-heavy bug or complex edge cases that require deep “reasoning,” toggle this on.
Automation & Customization
This is where it gets nerdy. You aren’t stuck with the default commands. You can create a commands
folder, add a .md
file, and then just use a /filename
command to run whatever you wrote in that file.
Another neat integration Claude Code has is with GitHub Actions. You can set it up to automatically monitor issues. When you call it using @claude, can you check this out?
, it springs into action. It will automatically run your code, check if the issue is actually occurring, suggest fixes, and then document its findings in the GitHub comment section.
The Safety Net: Hooks
The Hooks system is a power-user feature that allows custom code execution just before or after a tool is called.
If you’ve ever worked with Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) in Java, this will feel very familiar. Just like AOP allows you to “inject” behavior (like logging or security checks) without cluttering your core logic, Hooks let you programmatically intercept what Claude is doing via JavaScript functions. This is perfect for:
- Logging: Audit trails for every file change.
- File Access Control: Automatically blocking Claude if it tries to touch a
.env
file. - Validation: Ensuring code meets standards before it’s even saved.
Claude vs. Cursor: The “Daily Driver” Dilemma
As someone who daily drives Cursor for both personal and professional work, I found this comparison fascinating. They look similar, but have key differences:
- Cursor excels at day-to-day, focused tasks. If you want to add a button to your website, change a component, or you know exactly where the change needs to happen, Cursor is the gold standard. It’s built for speed and “in-the-flow” editing.
- Claude Code is built for complex project reasoning. It can easily read your entire codebase, and with its powerful modes, it can go wider and deeper than a standard IDE assistant might suggest.
Final Thoughts: The New Mandatory Baseline
Whether you prefer the surgical precision of Cursor or the architectural breadth of Claude Code, these tools are no longer just “nice-to-have” perks. In 2026, they are a necessity.
The productivity increase they bring is insane, and it is almost mandatory now for developers to learn and use them. We are moving from writing every line of code by hand to orchestrating agents that do the heavy lifting. While Cursor remains my go-to for the flow of building, Claude Code has earned a permanent spot in my toolkit for the “hard stuff” — the tasks that require a partner that truly understands the “why” behind the codebase.
